Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Fig. 1B and Materials and Methods explicitly list SDI1 as At5g48850.

Source_Species.reason: The paper studies SDI1 in Arabidopsis; WT is Arabidopsis accession Columbia (Col-0).

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Arabidopsis thaliana is explicitly stated throughout, including Materials and Methods.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: Materials and Methods states A. thaliana accession Columbia (Col-0) was used as WT.

Applied_Species.reason: Functional assays for SDI1 knockout, overexpression, expression reporters, and protoplast work were done in Arabidopsis.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Applied species in the core experiments is Arabidopsis thaliana.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: The Arabidopsis background used is Col-0 WT.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Results state SDI family proteins contain one TPR domain; Fig. 1 notes SDI1 has a putative nuclear localization signal, and SDI1 is sulfur deficiency induced.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results show SDI1/SDI2 repress GSL biosynthesis; sdi1 loss increases GSL genes and GSL accumulation, SDI1 overexpression lowers them. Abstract and Discussion state this supports prioritization of sulfate usage for primary metabolites under sulfur deprivation. The primary-metabolism prioritization part is more interpretive but explicitly stated by authors.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed methods appear in Results/Methods: T-DNA mutants, overexpression, qRT-PCR, GSL profiling, microarray/PCA, localization, Y2H, BiFC, transactivation assay, and EMSA.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: Each listed point is supported: induction by -S/OAS and tissue pattern (Fig. 1, fig. S3), increased GSL genes/GSLs in mutants (Figs. 2, 6), repression in overexpressors (Figs. 3, 6), nucleus/cytosol localization and MYB28 interaction (Fig. 7), and inhibition of MYB28 transactivation without loss of DNA binding (Fig. 8).

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: The measured end products are glucosinolates (aliphatic, indolic, total) in shoots and roots.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Introduction explicitly calls GSLs sulfur-rich secondary metabolites.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: GSL contents were measured in shoots and roots. Vascular association is supported for SDI expression and discussed for pathway gene expression.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Introduction states GSLs have antipathogenic/antiherbivory roles and medicinal, carcinopreventive, and antibiotic activities.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: Fig. 7A shows SDI1-GFP/GFP-SDI1 signals in nucleus and cytosol.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Results and fig. S3 state strong induction in roots under -S; GUS in vascular tissues of roots/shoots, shoots, stems, flower buds, stigma, stamens; not detected in mature siliques.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: For Arabidopsis, SDI1 is induced in roots/vascular tissues under sulfur deficiency and by OAS treatment.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: Y2H showed interaction with MYB28, MYB29, and MYB76 in yeast, but BiFC in plant cells supported only MYB28. Listing all three without qualification overstates in planta support.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Methods specify CaMV 35S promoter for SDI1 overexpression and native SDI1 promoter for GFP/GUS reporter constructs.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: The study uses sulfur deficiency defined as 15 µM sulfate versus 1500 µM sulfate and also OAS treatment for promoter:GUS experiments.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Results show reduced SDI activity in mutants is associated with lower GSH/Cys and higher GSLs; Discussion explicitly mentions growth/energy cost of GSL production. This is a reasoned tradeoff drawn by authors.

Omics_Data.reason: Methods specify Affymetrix ATH1 array and GEO accession GSE81347.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: Discussion explicitly suggests breeding for reduced leaf SDI1/SDI2 activities to increase or sustain GSL contents/quality; altered sulfur metabolite balance is supported by metabolite data.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Paper states SDI1 is the major component versus SDI2 and acts through protein-protein interaction with MYB28 rather than direct DNA binding.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Fig. 1B and Methods explicitly list SDI2 as At1g04770.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Arabidopsis thaliana is the study species.

Applied_Species.reason: Core functional analyses were done in Arabidopsis.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: SDI2 is part of the SDI family containing one TPR domain and is sulfur-deficiency induced. Unlike SDI1, the paper says SDI2 lacks the putative NLS.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results conclude SDI2 can be an additively functional form contributing to down-regulation of GSL biosynthesis, weaker than SDI1.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed methods for SDI2 appear: T-DNA mutant, double knockout with sdi1, 35S overexpression, qRT-PCR, GSL profiling, promoter:GFP/GUS expression.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All points are supported: induction by -S/OAS with vascular/root expression, weaker single-mutant phenotype than sdi1, weaker repression by overexpression than SDI1, and additive role with SDI1.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: The linked final products are glucosinolates.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: GSLs are explicitly sulfur-rich secondary metabolites.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Shoot and root GSLs were measured for SDI2 genotypes.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: GSL defense and medicinal functions are stated in the Introduction, though not specific to SDI2.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Results show SDI2 is induced by -S in roots and vascular tissues; reporter intensity is lower than SDI1 and restricted mainly to vasculature.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Arabidopsis SDI2 expression is induced in roots/vascular tissues under -S and by OAS treatment.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Methods specify CaMV 35S for overexpression and native SDI2 promoter for GFP/GUS reporter constructs.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Sulfur deficiency and OAS treatment are explicitly used.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Data show stronger GSH/Cys decreases in combined SDI1/SDI2 disruption with enhanced GSLs; thus the statement about reduced SDI2 together with SDI1 is supported.

Omics_Data.reason: ATH1 microarray and GEO GSE81347 are reported, although the detailed microarray design centered on SDI1ox and sdi1sdi2.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: Discussion proposes reduced SDI1/SDI2 activities to enhance GSL contents; effect of SDI2 alone is clearly smaller in Results.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Results/Discussion support that SDI2 is weaker/additive versus SDI1; Arabidopsis SDI1/SDI2 belong to a Brassicaceae-specific subbranch, suggesting specialized function.

Source_Species.reason: MYB28 studied here is the Arabidopsis factor involved in aliphatic GSL biosynthesis.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The experiments and referenced pathway are in Arabidopsis thaliana.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: Context of Arabidopsis experiments is Col-0 background.

Applied_Species.reason: MYB28 functional assays include Arabidopsis cultured cells/protoplast context, though BiFC used N. benthamiana for interaction visualization.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Core regulatory context is Arabidopsis thaliana.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: Arabidopsis background used in the study is Col-0.

Regulator_Type.reason: MYB28 is explicitly described as a transcription factor.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Discussion explicitly refers to MYB28 as an R2R3-MYB type transcription factor.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Paper states MYB28 is a major transcription factor promoting aliphatic GSL biosynthesis, and SDI1 represses its activity leading to lower aliphatic GSL biosynthesis.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Y2H, BiFC, transient transactivation assays, EMSA, and transcript profiling are all present in Results.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: Each point is supported by cited Results figures and transcript data, though feedback circuitry is partly discussed using prior literature plus this paper's transcript data.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: MYB28 is linked specifically to aliphatic glucosinolates in this paper.

Metabolic_Process_Controlled.reason: MYB28 regulates biosynthesis of aliphatic glucosinolates.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: GSLs were measured in shoots and roots, and vascular association is discussed, but MYB28-specific evidence for accumulation sites in this paper is indirect.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: General functions of glucosinolates are stated in the paper.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: BiFC detected SDI1-MYB28 interaction in nuclei, implying MYB28 nuclear presence in that assay, but the paper did not directly perform a standalone MYB28 localization experiment.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: The vascular-tissue preference is cited from previous studies in Discussion, not directly tested here; the statement that MYB28 is up-regulated by sulfur deficiency is supported by Fig. 6A.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: This is mostly based on discussion and prior reports rather than direct expression assays for MYB28 in this paper.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Methods state MYB28 fused to the CaMV 35S promoter in pGWB2 was used as effector in transactivation assays.

Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs.reason: Results/Discussion support sulfur deficiency effects via SDI1/SDI2 on MYB28 activity and transcript levels; positive regulatory relationships with MYB29/MYB76 and autoregulation are discussed from prior work.

Protein_Complex_Involvement.reason: BiFC and EMSA/transactivation support formation of an SDI1-MYB28 complex in the nucleus.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: Direct interaction shown here is with SDI1. MYB29 and MYB76 are discussed as part of regulatory network, but direct MYB28-MYB29/MYB76 protein interactions were not shown in this paper.

Regulation_Mode.reason: MYB28 activates aliphatic GSL genes, and this activity is inhibited by SDI1 protein-protein interaction.

Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes.reason: Transactivation assays directly show activation of CYP79F1 and CYP83A1 by MYB28; BCAT4 and MAM1 are part of aliphatic GSL genes negatively associated with SDI1 and linked to MYB28 control in pathway summaries.

Primary_Regulatory_Targets.reason: CYP79F1 and CYP83A1 are directly assayed here; BCAT4 and MAM1 are plausible targets in the pathway. MYB29, MYB76, and MYB28 self-regulation are discussed from previous studies, not directly tested in this paper.

Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation.reason: This field relies heavily on prior reports and speculation; the paper discusses but does not directly test the feedback loops experimentally here.

Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product.reason: The paper explicitly describes MYB28 as the major transcription factor promoting aliphatic GSL biosynthesis and the direct functional target of SDI1 repression.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: While not directly tested for MYB28 manipulation here, the paper discusses sulfur/energy costs of GSL production; because MYB28 promotes aliphatic GSL biosynthesis, this inference is reasonable from the paper.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper explicitly proposes SDI1/SDI2 manipulation for breeding, but not direct MYB28 engineering strategies in its own experimental conclusions.

Omics_Data.reason: MYB28 transcript behavior appears in the ATH1 microarray/GSE81347 data presented in Fig. 6/table S8.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Sulfur deficiency is the environmental context affecting MYB28 transcript/activity in this study.

Other_Important_Info.reason: EMSA and transactivation data support that SDI1 does not abolish MYB28 DNA binding and repression likely occurs after binding, consistent with the authors' interpretation.

Applied_Species.reason: The discussed slim1 mutant data are in Arabidopsis.

Regulator_Type.reason: Discussion explicitly refers to SLIM1 as a transcription factor.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: This paper does not state that SLIM1 is an EIL-family regulator, even if known from literature.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: The paper discusses SLIM1 as a broad sulfur-deficiency regulator linked to SDI1 and GSL down-regulation, but SLIM1 was not directly tested in Results of this paper.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: The field accurately says this is inferred from previously published microarray analysis of slim1 mutants discussed in this paper.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: These points reflect discussion and prior work rather than direct core findings from this paper's Results; the proposed SLIM1-mediated pathway is explicitly framed as a model/speculation.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: SLIM1 is linked to glucosinolate regulation contextually, but not directly tested here as a gene-to-product experiment.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Glucosinolates are sulfur-rich secondary metabolites in this paper.

Metabolic_Process_Controlled.reason: This is a discussion-level interpretation of SLIM1's role, not directly established by Results in this paper.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: General GSL defense/medicinal functions are stated in the paper.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Discussion explicitly states SLIM1 is not transcriptionally regulated in response to sulfur deficiency.

Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs.reason: Discussion explicitly links SLIM1 to sulfur deficiency and suggests posttranslational modifications may be necessary.

Regulation_Mode.reason: Discussion describes SLIM1 as a regulatory factor affecting SDI1 expression under +S and -S conditions.

Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes.reason: The paper explicitly says SDI1 gene expression is affected by slim1 and references GSL-related genes altered in slim1 mutants.

Primary_Regulatory_Targets.reason: SDI1 is explicitly discussed as being affected by SLIM1.

Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product.reason: This is a proposed upstream connection in the Discussion, not a directly demonstrated gene-to-product effect in this paper's Results.

Omics_Data.reason: The paper references previous microarray data in slim1 mutants and notes accession is not provided here; this description matches the text.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Sulfur deficiency is the key condition discussed for SLIM1.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper indeed treats SLIM1 as upstream context rather than a core experimentally tested subject in the Results.

corrections.reason: This paper does not state that SLIM1 is an EIL-family regulator, even if known from literature.
